messages.content: i want to host a beta minecraft server

Great! Here are some steps you can follow to host a beta Minecraft server:

1. Download the Minecraft server software from the official Minecraft website.

2. Once downloaded, create a new folder on your computer where you want to host the server. This will be where all server files will be saved.

3. Next, open the server software and configure the server settings, such as server name, max players, game mode, and any mods or plugins you want to use.

4. Forward the server port on your router so that players can connect to your server. The default port for Minecraft server is 25565.

5. Invite players to join your beta Minecraft server and test it out to see if there are any bugs or issues that need to be fixed.

6. Keep the server updated with the latest version of Minecraft and any plugins or mods you're using.

7. Have fun and enjoy playing Minecraft with others!
im renting a server but its only 1 gb so its infredibly cheap

That's great! However, keep in mind that a low amount of RAM may limit the number of players that can join your server and the size of your world, and also may increase the likelihood of lag and crashes. Here are some tips to optimize your server's performance:

1. Optimize server settings: reduce view distance, disable unnecessary plugins, and limit entity spawns.

2. Configure server start script: allocate more RAM to the server, adjust Java arguments, and install a server optimization mod like Paper or Spigot.

3. Limit player count: if your server has a small amount of RAM, limit the number of concurrent players by setting up a whitelist or password.

4. Clean up server files: regularly delete unused chunks, logs, and other server data to reduce the server's memory usage.

5. Consult with your hosting provider: some hosting companies offer additional features like SSD disks, DDoS protection, and automatic backups that can help improve server performance.

Remember, a cheap server may not offer you the best gaming experience, but you can still enjoy playing with your friends or small groups by optimizing the server settings and proper server management.
beta mc barely even uses 200 mb of ram

That's true, the amount of RAM usage in a beta Minecraft server can be relatively low compared to other Minecraft versions. However, keep in mind that the amount of RAM required depends on the size of your world, the number of concurrent players, and the mods or plugins you're using. 

Even if your server is using less than 200MB of RAM, it's still important to monitor the server's performance to avoid potential issues and optimize your gaming experience. You can use server monitoring tools like Minecraft Server Monitor or MC Stats to check your server's metrics, or consider installing server plugins like NoLagg or ClearLagg to reduce lag and optimize server performance.
running mc beta 1.7.3

Beta 1.7.3 is an older version of Minecraft, released in June 2011. It has lower system requirements compared to newer Minecraft versions, which means it can run on older or less powerful computer systems. Here are some tips to optimize your performance when running MC Beta 1.7.3:

1. Install OptiFine or other performance-enhancing mods. These can improve the game's frame rate, reduce lag, and optimize graphics settings.

2. Limit the number of concurrent players. Since MC Beta 1.7.3 is an older version, it may not be able to handle a large number of players, so consider setting up a whitelist or password.

3. Allocate more RAM to the server. Even if the game uses less RAM overall, having more memory dedicated to the server can help improve its performance.

4. Use a performance-optimized server wrapper like Paper to improve overall server performance.

5. Keep your server up to date with the latest version of MC Beta 1.7.3. This can help fix bugs, improve performance, and optimize features. 

6. Close any unnecessary applications or background processes to free up system resources and improve performance.

By following these tips, you can optimize your performance when running MC Beta 1.7.3 and enjoy playing Minecraft on an older version.
I'm sorry, but it seems like you didn't provide any input for me to work with. Could you please rephrase your question or provide additional information? I'm here to help!
ram needed for beta mc

The amount of RAM needed for running a beta Minecraft server varies depending on the version of Minecraft, the number of players, and the size of the world. For Beta versions of Minecraft, generally, the RAM required is lower compared to newer versions.

Beta versions of Minecraft were released in the early stages of the game and have lower system requirements. For example, Minecraft Beta 1.7.3 requires approximately 256MB-512MB of RAM to run, depending on the amount of content and mods added.

However, it's important to keep in mind that adding mods or plugins, larger worlds, or more concurrent players can increase the RAM usage of the server. Therefore, adding more RAM to the server will help with the overall performance of the server and prevent potential issues such as lag or crashes. 

For a server running Minecraft Beta, I would recommend a minimum of 1GB of RAM to ensure that it runs smoothly.
its vanilla beta

If you're running a vanilla beta Minecraft server, then the amount of RAM needed will depend on the number of players and the size of your world. In general, a vanilla Minecraft server with a moderate amount of players should work fine with 512MB-1GB of RAM allocated to the server.

However, keep in mind that even a vanilla server can run into performance issues or lag if there are too many entities, redstone machines, or structures in the game. Therefore, it's a good idea to monitor the server's performance and allocate more RAM if necessary.

Additionally, make sure that you have the latest version of the server software installed, as older versions can have performance and stability issues. You can download the latest version of the Minecraft server software from the official Minecraft website.
running a beta minecraft server off my computer (amd ryzen 5 5600g with 16 gb of ram)

Your computer's specifications should be more than enough to run a beta Minecraft server with good performance. A Minecraft server with a moderate amount of players should work fine with 1GB or more of allocated RAM. With 16GB of RAM, you'll have plenty of memory for running the server and other applications. 

However, if you plan to run other applications simultaneously, make sure you leave enough RAM to the server to ensure smooth performance. It's also a good idea to allocate additional RAM to the server if you plan to use mods or plugins on your server.

Keep in mind that running a server on your own computer can impact your computer's performance, since it will be using processing power and RAM resources. To optimize performance, make sure to close any unnecessary applications or background processes and monitor the server's performance to ensure that it's running smoothly.
how many players can i have on with 16 gb

The number of players that you can have on a Minecraft server with 16 GB of RAM will depend on various factors such as the Minecraft version, server configuration, and world size.

For a Vanilla Minecraft server with 16GB of RAM, you should be able to have around 80-100 players or more, depending on the world size, player activity, and server configuration. However, if you plan to use mods or plugins, this number might be lower, depending on the resource requirements of the mods or plugins.

It is important to keep in mind that other factors such as internet speed and server CPU performance also affect the number of players that can join the Minecraft Server. While having 16GB of memory is a good amount, keep in mind that too many concurrent players can affect your internet connection and cause lag or other performance issues.

Ultimately, the best way to determine the maximum number of players that your server can handle is to test with a smaller group of players initially and monitor the server's performance. You can gradually increase the number of players over time as you identify and resolve any potential issues.
